tweets.content:
Most people think "hardware-encrypted SSD" and "TCG Opal" mean the same thing.

They don't.

A Self-Encrypting Drive (SED) encrypts data in hardware. TCG Opal is an open industry standard that defines how software securely provisions, authenticates, locks, unlocks, and manages that encrypted storage.

The encryption itself happens inside the SSD controller.

As data is written to NAND flash, the controller encrypts it transparently using media encryption keys that remain within the drive. Reads are decrypted just as transparently after the drive has been successfully unlocked.

The host doesn't encrypt every sector when Opal hardware encryption is being used.

Instead, the operating system or management software communicates with the drive using the Opal protocol to configure security policies, authenticate users, and control access to protected data.

One of Opal's most useful features is Locking Ranges.

Instead of protecting the entire drive as a single encrypted volume, Opal allows independent ranges of logical block addresses (LBAs) to be locked or unlocked separately. This enables different regions of the same drive to have different access policies.

Authentication determines whether the drive will make protected data accessible.

The Opal specification defines the authentication and authorization framework but intentionally does not mandate exactly how vendors implement their internal cryptographic key hierarchy. Those implementation details are left to SSD manufacturers.

Another powerful capability is cryptographic erase.

Because data is already encrypted, securely sanitizing a drive doesn't necessarily require overwriting every NAND cell. By replacing or destroying the encryption keys protecting the stored data, previously written ciphertext becomes computationally infeasible to recover.

Opal also supports Pre-Boot Authentication (PBA).

When deployed, the drive remains locked before the operating system starts, helping protect against offline attacks where an attacker removes the SSD and attempts to access it from another machine.

Since encryption and decryption are performed by dedicated hardware in the SSD controller, the CPU doesn't perform the bulk cryptographic work during normal I/O. The host primarily handles authentication, policy management, and storage commands.

One important caveat:

The security of an Opal-based system depends on both the specification and the vendor's implementation.

In 2018, researchers showed that several commercially available self-encrypting SSDs contained implementation flaws that could undermine their security even though they supported hardware encryption.

A good specification is only half the story.

A secure implementation is what ultimately protects your data.

tweets.content:
I wrote an article about my sentiment: https://t.co/wfoyJnVurq.

I'm all for open source. I've shipped plenty of it, and I'll keep doing it* . 

But let's be honest about words. "Vibe coding" is when you sit down with a blank file and build something from your own ideas. It's *not* pointing your AI agent at someone else's finished architecture and saying "re-implement this under a new name." Same when you say: "inspired by" and still point your AI at an existing, time tested code base, script or other IP. "inspired by" is dishonest, when done in that fashion.

That second thing has a name too. It's called copying. Stripping the attribution doesn't make it yours, and calling it "vibe coding" doesn't make it fair use.

Reverse-engineer my work to learn from it? great, that's the culture. But at least have the honesty to say what you did, and leave proper credit where it's due.

tweets.content:
RT @newsbrd_de: 🔐 Nationale Sicherheit: Pistorius will AfD von Geheimdienstinformationen fernhalten

Bundesverteidigungsminister Boris Pistorius (SPD) will verhindern, dass die AfD bei einer möglichen Regierungsbeteiligung Zugang zu sensiblen Geheimdienstinformationen erhält. Als Begründung verweist er auf die enge Nähe der Partei zu Russland.

'Die Nähe zu Putin ist nicht zu übersehen. Die Vermutung, dass es Geld aus Russland gibt, steht ebenfalls im Raum', sagte Pistorius der 'Bild am Sonntag'. Sensible Informationen dürften nicht in die falschen Hände geraten.

Pistorius: 'Allergrößte Sorgen' um Demokratie

Der Minister betonte, man beschäftige sich intensiv mit der Frage, wem Zugang zu geheim eingestuften Informationen gewährt werden könne. 'Dazu sind wir verpflichtet, weil es um die Sicherheit unseres Landes geht', so Pistorius.

Besonders besorgt zeigte sich der SPD-Politiker über mögliche Wahlergebnisse: 'Ich mache mir als Demokrat über einen solchen möglichen Wahlausgang allergrößte Sorgen.' Mehrere Landesverbände der AfD gelten beim Verfassungsschutz als gesichert rechtsextrem.

AfD-Parteitag in Erfurt

Parallel dazu beendete die AfD am Sonntag ihren von Protesten begleiteten Parteitag in Erfurt. Am Samstag waren Alice Weidel und Tino Chrupalla als Parteivorsitzende in ihren Ämtern bestätigt worden.

Auf die Frage, was er einem AfD-unterstützenden Soldaten raten würde, antwortete Pistorius: 'Ich würde ihm sagen: Überlegen Sie sich das gut im Sinne unserer Demokratie. Aber es ist am Ende Ihre Entscheidung, wen Sie wählen.'

tweets.content:
RT @cr3ghost: Everyone talks about using AI for vulnerability research. Nobody talks about the orchestration layer that makes it actually work.

This post covers the harness: how to structure LLM pipelines for security research so they produce consistent, verifiable findings instead of burning tokens on hallucinated garbage.

Reviews RAPTOR, Anthropic's reference harness, Baby Naptime, Evil Socket's Audit, and Visa's VVAH. Covers context budgets, model routing, RAG for persistent memory, validation gates, and why one system prompt for the entire pipeline is a mistake.

Released an open-source template to build your own.

Blog: https://t.co/ivRzHAMqED 
GitHub: https://t.co/h3skHfdpf7

Microsoft records showed the device hitting the ngrok signup page at the exact minute the attack account was created, then surfacing on home IPs in Tallinn, New York, and Thailand.

He used a VPN during the intrusion. The device ID followed him anyway.

tweets.content:
This is why memory forensics has become one of the most valuable skills in modern incident response.

Unlike traditional forensic analysis, memory forensics allows investigators to examine what is happening while the system is running. That means you can uncover malicious processes, injected code, active network connections, decrypted payloads, command-line arguments, and even credentials that may never be written to disk.

In this investigation, a suspicious process immediately stands out. By examining its memory, investigators can identify embedded strings, API endpoints, hidden PowerShell commands, and other indicators that reveal how the malware communicates and what it is trying to achieve.

One thing every defender should remember is that RAM is volatile. Once a compromised system is shut down, a significant amount of valuable evidence disappears forever. That’s why experienced incident responders prioritize memory acquisition before containment whenever it’s safe to do so.

Malware can hide files, clear logs, and remove persistence, but it still has to exist in memory while it’s running. If you want to understand what really happened during an attack, memory is often where the truth is hiding.

quotedTweet.rawContent:
I GOTCHU, Now lets get answering 

Question 1: Redesigning the Network

The flat network is the core problem. One infected guest device has unobstructed access to every server. That is not a design flaw. That is the exact lateral movement path ransomware uses.

Fix it with four VLANs minimum.

Server VLAN with strict ACLs permitting only what each server actually needs. User VLAN that reaches servers only through defined permitted paths. Guest VLAN with internet access only, zero internal visibility. Management VLAN for infrastructure devices that no user workstation can touch.

The NAT firewall goes next. NAT to the internet provides zero protection against internal east-west traffic. Replace it with something capable of stateful inspection and inter-VLAN filtering. Then add IDS/IPS because you cannot defend what you cannot see.

Priority order: segmentation, firewall rules that enforce it, visibility through monitoring, everything else.

Question 2: Common SMB Misconfigurations

Default credentials on switches, routers, access points, and cameras. Audit everything before anything else.

RDP exposed directly to the internet on port 3389. Disable it or put it behind a VPN with MFA. This is one of the primary ransomware entry points in documented SMB incidents for five consecutive years.

SMBv1 still enabled. Check with Get-SmbServerConfiguration in PowerShell. Disable it immediately if present.

Overprivileged service accounts with Domain Admin membership turning a single compromised credential into a full domain takeover.

No monitoring meaning attackers dwell undetected for weeks before deploying ransomware.

Run Nessus Essentials or OpenVAS internally. The results will be uncomfortable. Fix criticals first.

The pattern is always the same. Entry, lateral movement across a flat network, privilege escalation, total deployment. Each step has a specific control that breaks the chain. This network has none of them.

Hope this helped.

quotedTweet.rawContent: During Windows 3.1's installation setup, Microsoft embedded self-modifying XOR-encrypted code that secretly checked whether the machine was running DR-DOS instead of MS-DOS. If it detected a competitor's OS, it deliberately triggered a fake error screen to scare users away. The engineer who wrote it embedded his own initials in the encrypted signature. It was discovered years later during antitrust litigation. Microsoft paid $280M to settle.

tweets.content:
For years, people assumed pulling the power plug instantly erased everything in RAM. It doesn't.

That simple assumption led to one of the most influential hardware security papers ever published: Cold Boot Attacks.

The key observation was that DRAM doesn't lose its contents immediately after power is removed. It stores data as electrical charge in tiny capacitors, and those charges leak away gradually. At room temperature, memory can retain data for several seconds. Cool the DIMMs, and that window can extend significantly.

Researchers showed that an attacker with physical access could exploit this behavior by quickly rebooting a machine into a small custom OS or even transplanting the DIMMs into another compatible system, and dumping memory before the contents fully decayed.

Why is that useful?

Because RAM often contains things you never want an attacker to see:
• AES disk encryption keys
• RSA private keys
• Kernel secrets
• User credentials
• Session keys

The clever part wasn't just dumping memory.

Even after some bits had decayed, the researchers developed reconstruction techniques that recovered valid cryptographic keys from imperfect memory images. They successfully demonstrated attacks against systems using BitLocker, FileVault, dm-crypt, and TrueCrypt (in configurations where the encryption keys were already resident in RAM).

The paper changed an important security assumption.

The cryptography wasn't broken.
The operating system wasn't broken.

The weakness came from a physical property of DRAM itself.

It's a reminder that computer security isn't just about software bugs or weak algorithms. Sometimes, the behavior of the hardware underneath becomes part of the attack surface.

tweets.content:
Similar to game cheats vs anti-cheats, understanding the offensive side first is how many of the best detection engineers and anti-cheat engineers started. If you work in detection engineering or malware analysis, this interview is worth watching.

@C5pider  talks about his journey and how working with @0xBoku  influenced his approach to building adversary emulation tooling. His open-source work (Havoc, Stardust, Ekko, KaynLdr) inspired an entire generation of security researchers to build their own projects, credited or not. Raphael Mudge, the creator of Cobalt Strike, endorsed his work and recognized its contribution to the offensive security community. The same way kids on UnKnoWnCheaTs turned their game hacking projects into careers at anti-cheat firms, his work motivated younger developers to go from hobby projects to professional offensive and defensive security.

Understanding how implant developers think about CET compliant call stack spoofing, sleep obfuscation, stack duplication during sleep, indirect syscalls for Nt* APIs, return address spoofing, AMSI/ETW patching via hardware breakpoints, proxy library loading, malleable C2 profiles, position-independent shellcode, RISC-V VM-based obfuscation, EDR blinding, token vaults, SMB and TCP P2P channels, and cross-platform implants across Windows, Linux, macOS, and AArch64 gives you detection opportunities that signatures alone will never catch.

Learning from people building these professionally is how you write better detection rules.

#DetectionEngineering #MalwareAnalysis #InfoSec

tweets.content:
This dashboard gives security analysts a real-time view of what’s happening across an enterprise. Instead of looking at thousands of individual events, the SOC can immediately identify trends, high-priority incidents, geographic attack sources, and the types of threats targeting the environment.

One thing I always tell people is this: an alert is not an incident. A brute-force attack, a malware detection, or an unusual authentication event may all be related. The job of a SOC analyst is to correlate those events, determine what actually happened, and decide whether it’s a real compromise or just normal activity.

Notice how this dashboard breaks down alert severity, attack categories, timelines, and source IPs. That allows analysts to prioritize critical threats first, understand whether attacks are increasing over time, and quickly identify systems that require immediate attention.

A SOC isn’t about watching dashboards all day. It’s about turning millions of logs into meaningful intelligence that helps defenders detect, investigate, and stop attacks before they become breaches.

tweets.content:
RT @ptdbugs: Insights from Compromise Assessment projects: missed incidents and hidden threats

Kaspersky researchers analyzed the results of Compromise Assessment projects conducted in 2025 and identified key reasons why attackers can remain undetected in corporate infrastructures for months. Below are the main findings — the full report is available at this link -> (https://t.co/cL2cUml7a5).

60% of incidents were not detected by existing security tools before the Compromise Assessment began. About 20% of threats were identified only through manual analysis by specialists.

More than half (52%) of high‑severity incidents showed traces of compromise for over 90 days. In one case, attackers remained in the infrastructure for about four years, using compromised domain controllers for covert cryptomining.

40% of detected web shells were found in backups, creating a risk of re‑compromise after infrastructure restoration.

86% of organizations that claimed to have a vulnerability management process still had exploitable misconfigurations. The authors attribute this to a lack of formalized vulnerability management processes and incomplete asset inventory: in 25% of cases individual servers were not monitored at all.

In every project that revealed an incident, attackers used remote‑administration tools and Living off the Land (LoLBins) techniques to disguise their activity as legitimate OS processes.

The researchers also note that organizations conducting Compromise Assessments regularly are far less likely to face high‑severity incidents than those turning to such audits only after an attack.

The study demonstrates that the absence of detections should not be taken as proof of security. In practice, what matters most is not just having monitoring tools but overall operational maturity: proper configuration of defenses, continuous monitoring, proactive threat hunting, accurate asset inventory, and analysts’ readiness to investigate even low‑priority events. Equally important are well‑defined communication processes and regularly updated incident response plans — these factors enable timely detection of hidden compromises and help reduce the risk of critical incidents.

#dbugs_analytics

RT @DailyDarkWeb: 🚨 A threat actor is claiming to auction a pre-authentication Microsoft 365 initial access exploit allegedly targeting core Exchange Online infrastructure.

According to the listing, the alleged exploit provides:
* Pre-authentication, zero-click initial access
* SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery)
* Access to internal Exchange Online infrastructure
* OWA session cookie generation
* Cross-tenant account takeover capabilities
* Bypass of perimeter protections and segmentation

The seller claims:
* Starting bid: $1,000,000 (Monero/XMR)
* Buy Now: $2,500,000
* Live verification available through forum escrow

At the time of writing, there is no public evidence supporting these claims, and the alleged vulnerability has not been independently verified.

Analyst Note: High-profile exploit advertisements targeting major cloud providers are common on underground forums and often contain exaggerated or fraudulent claims. However, listings involving widely deployed enterprise platforms deserve close monitoring until validated or disproven by vendor investigations. Organizations should continue following Microsoft's security advisories, enforce MFA and Conditional Access policies, and monitor for unusual authentication activity.

tweets.content:
Just clearing up a few things about TI ETW and call stack capturing.
If you're subscribed to Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence or Security-Mitigations and assuming a call stack just comes with the event, check again. Went through 24 of these events in ntoskrnl. Exactly one hardcodes a stack into its own payload. The other 23 are opt-in, and the mechanism for turning it on lives nowhere near the event functions themselves.
It's in the shared write backend, EtwpEventWriteFull, not in any individual event. It only runs if your session sets EnableProperty |= EVENT_ENABLE_PROPERTY_STACK_TRACE in EnableTraceEx2. Skip that and all 23 ship with nothing attached, silently, no error, no warning.
You don't have to eat the cost across the whole provider either. Scope it to specific event IDs with an EVENT_FILTER_TYPE_STACKWALK filter (EVENT_FILTER_EVENT_ID struct). Kernel side, that's EtwpApplyStackWalkIdFilter doing a hash lookup per event ID before it bothers walking anything at all.
Once it's on, capture is synchronous almost every time. EtwWrite blocks right there in the call, walks the stack via RtlWalkFrameChain, fills the buffer, and returns. That holds as long as IRQL stays below DISPATCH_LEVEL and the thread can still take a page fault. That covers most of the 24.
The exception is IRQL and fault safety, not which thread the event is about. Above DISPATCH_LEVEL, mid an attach to another process's address space, or any state where the thread can't safely fault, the synchronous path breaks. When it does, the event ships immediately with an empty or partial stack instead of blocking. But the kernel doesn't just drop it. It tries to queue a real kernel APC, EtwpStackWalkApc, to finish the walk later.
And it really is async, not a disguised synchronous call. The paired routine, EtwpCancelStackWalkApc, is the KAPC's RundownRoutine. the callback that only fires if the thread exits before the APC gets to run. 
If the APC succeeds, you don't get a late version of the original event. You get a whole separate one, ETW_EVENT_USER_STACK_TRACE, correlated back via a MatchId field. If queuing fails or one's already pending, the kernel just gives up and clears MatchId. No correlation, no retry.
Even when it works, the stack isn't sitting in the payload you normally parse. It's a separate Extended Data Item on the record(ExtType 6 for raw addresses, or 18) if stack-key caching is on and you only get a 64-bit key back instead. That's TdhGetEventInformation territory, not your usual field parser.
Here’s a quick look at the internal call flow.
